text: These are some quick tips from a case I recently performed.
This gentleman has a neuroendocrine tumor in the pancreas and is undergoing a laparoscopic distal pancreatectomy.
The surgeon mobilizes the short gastric vessels and opens the lesser sac, revealing a significant amount of intra-abdominal fat.
The laparoscopic view reveals a significant amount of adipose tissue overlaying the pancreas, which is being exposed during the distal pancreatectomy procedure.
The surgeon carefully identifies the splenic vessels located posteriorly to the pancreas, aiming to preserve them during the laparoscopic distal pancreatectomy.
The laparoscopic view reveals the splenic vein as the surgeon continues the dissection around the pancreas, with visible intra-abdominal fat surrounding the area.
The surgeon skeletonizes the tissue laterally around the pancreas, with the patient positioned in full left decubitus to facilitate the dissection.
The surgeon identifies the tail of the pancreas, which appears atrophic and is located distantly from the splenic hilum, amidst challenging dissection conditions.
The surgeon encounters difficulty identifying the tumor due to the substantial amount of fat covering the pancreas.
In cases like this, performing intraoperative laparoscopic ultrasound can be very helpful.
So that's what we did here.
The surgeon uses an ultrasound probe to identify the tumor located more posteriorly within the pancreas gland.
The surgeon begins the process of skeletonizing the pancreas, focusing on carefully exposing and isolating the anatomical structures surrounding the gland.

The surgeon exposes the splenic artery and vein, which have been dissected, using a harmonic device to take remaining attachments. The surgical field shows slight oozing controlled with Surgicel, and the lesion is stapled out once proximal to it.
This was a tricky case due to the intra-abdominal fat and the fatty pancreas but it went very well and the patient thankfully has done very well as well.
The spleen is visible, showing good perfusion overall, with a small area of ischemia observed at the superior pole.
